content.problemStatement:
For a permutation PPP of the integers [1,N][1, N][1,N], let QQQ be the unique inverse permutation of PPP (i.e. QPi=iQ_{P_i} = iQPi​​=i).

Define f(P)=∑i=1N−1[Pi<Pi+1]+∑i=1N−1[Qi>Qi+1]f(P) = \sum_{i = 1}^{N - 1}[P_i < P_{i + 1}] + \sum_{i = 1}^{N - 1}[Q_i > Q_{i + 1}]f(P)=∑i=1N−1​[Pi​<Pi+1​]+∑i=1N−1​[Qi​>Qi+1​], where [X][X][X] is 111 if XXX is true, 000 otherwise.

In other words, f(P)f(P)f(P) is the sum of number of ascents in PPP and descents in QQQ.

Given NNN and KKK, your task is to find a permutation PPP of the integers [1,N][1, N][1,N] such that f(P)≥Kf(P) \ge Kf(P)≥K.

There are only 222 tests in this problem:


N=6,K=6N = 6, K = 6N=6,K=6 (sample test)
N=105,K=1.99⋅105N = 10^5, K = 1.99 \cdot 10^5N=105,K=1.99⋅105

Input Format

The first line contains 222 integers - NNN and KKK.


Output Format
Output NNN integers - P1,P2,P3,…,PNP_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,P3​,…,PN​, (1≤Pi≤N)(1 \le P_i \le N)(1≤Pi​≤N), a valid permutation of the integers [1,N][1, N][1,N] such that f(P)≥Kf(P) \ge Kf(P)≥K.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Here, P=[2,4,5,1,3,6]P = [2, 4, 5, 1, 3, 6]P=[2,4,5,1,3,6] has 444 ascents (i=1,2,4,5i = 1, 2, 4, 5i=1,2,4,5 satisfy Pi<Pi+1P_i < P_{i + 1}Pi​<Pi+1​). The inverse permutation Q=[4,1,5,2,3,6]Q = [4, 1, 5, 2, 3, 6]Q=[4,1,5,2,3,6] has 222 descents (i=1,3i = 1, 3i=1,3 satisfy Qi>Qi+1Q_i > Q_{i + 1}Qi​>Qi+1​).
Thus, the sum is 666 as required. Finding a permutation with f(P)=7f(P) = 7f(P)=7 would also work.
